abstract:
This report analyzes longitudinal trends in language proficiency and usage among the U.S. Hispanic population from 1980 to 2013, drawing on data from the U.S. Census Bureau and the American Community Survey. Findings reveal a significant increase in English proficiency, with the share of Latinos ages five and older who speak English proficiently reaching 68% in 2013, up from 59% in 2000. This shift is primarily driven by demurring immigration levels and the rapid growth of the U.S.-born Latino population, who now outnumber foreign-born Hispanics two-to-one and demonstrate high rates of English dominance.

Concurrently, while a record 35.8 million Hispanics speak Spanish at home due to overall population growth, the relative share of households using Spanish has begun to decline. The study further details how English proficiency varies significantly by nativity, age of arrival, and educational attainment. Notably, while the share of proficient English speakers remains stable among foreign-born adults, there has been a sharp rise in proficiency among foreign-born children. These linguistic transitions highlight a broader demographic evolution within the Hispanic community, reflecting a settle-in effect where U.S. births have surpassed international migration as the primary engine of population growth.
